---
title: "README"
format: html
editor: visual
target audience: Academic Community of Polar Marine Ecologists and Biologists 
---

## Distinguishing Behavioral State Allocations for Whales with Calves on the Antarctic Foraging Ground

### Motivating Question

We want to understand if whales with calves, which are presumed to be female and the mother of the calf, alter their activity budget on the foraging ground to account for the energetic demands of rearing offspring. More simply: how do humpback whales with a calf present allocate their time to various behavioral states during the foraging season near the Western Antarctic Peninsula, and how do these behavioral state allocations compare to those of whales without calves?

### Rationale for the Question

There has been extensive study of the foraging behavior of Antarctic humpback whales along the Western Antarctic Peninsula but little work done in the region on how this behavior varies amongst different whale demographics. We had the unique opportunity to tap into a decade-long dataset of CATS (Customized Animal Tracking Solutions) tag data gathered by the Biotelemetry and Behavioral Ecology Lab and collaborators. We are excited to be one of the first groups to look at the unique behavior of mothers and calves.

### Background

**Scientific Motivation:**

Understanding how animals allocate time between behavioral states is a central goal in behavioral ecology, particularly for large, migratory marine mammals whose foraging opportunities are spatially and temporally constrained. For humpback whales, the Antarctic foraging season is a critical period during which individuals must acquire sufficient energetic reserves to support migration and reproduction for the rest of the year. 

The primary objective of this chapter is to build on past analyses of whale behavioral states to establish a quantitative baseline for humpback whale behavior during the foraging season (cite Ari and Ross). Due to the seasonal and diel variation in prey availability and distribution, as well as a whale’s changing body condition, humpback whales are known to allocate different amounts of time to four main behavioral states depending on the time of the season: traveling, resting, foraging, and exploring (Table 2). Parameters for these state allocations will be based on Friedlaender et. al 2013. Most studies to date have focused on the foraging behavioral state, and little has been done to explore the variation in time allocation to other behavioral states over the course of the season. The data presents us with an opportunity to help identify latent states that may not be described yet, particularly those that might fall into the broad “exploring” bin. 

By characterizing time allocation between broad behavioral states and examining how activity budgets vary over time and among demographic groups, this chapter provides a necessary reference point for interpreting behavioral plasticity, disturbance sensitivity, and longer-term physiological consequences explored in a later dissertation chapter. Importantly, this chapter is designed as a baseline synthesis rather than a fine-scale mechanistic or kinematic study. Its emphasis is on robust, interpretable patterns of behavior at ecologically meaningful scales.

**Future Directions:**

-   It will be beneficial to explore the metabolic requirements of different activity states to better understand the fluctuation in energetic demand between behavioral states (look at breathing rates).

**Data Overview:**

For this analysis, we will be using high-resolution, multi-sensor data collected via CATS tags from 2012-2025 during the Antarctic foraging season. Sixty-five tag deployments from 2015 - 2022 were found on the BTBEL database (Figure 1); tag deployments from subsequent years will need to be added from the Goldbogen lab database. Data streams include depth, movement, and orientation measurements sufficient to classify broad behavioral states and quantify temporal patterns in activity.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

**Figure 1: CATS Tag Deployments by Month and Year on Antarctic humpback whales**

Only deployments meeting established quality and duration criteria are included in the activity budget analyses, ensuring consistency and comparability across individuals and years.

**Behavioral Classification Framework:**

Behavioral state classification parameters are shown below in Table 1. These states were defined to capture major modes of behavior while maintaining interpretability across individuals and deployments. The “exploring” category functions as a residual state, encompassing active, non-directional behavior that is neither clearly foraging nor transit-oriented.

**Table 1: Description of and parameters for distinguishing behavioral states**

![](images/clipboard-2135873011.png)

**Update to Behavioral State Allocation – Further Analysis of “Exploring”**

Recent discussions have highlighted the importance of assessing whether the exploratory behavioral state encompasses multiple dive types distinguishable by basic dive characteristics (e.g., depth and duration). We will conduct a constrained, descriptive analysis to evaluate potential heterogeneity within the “exploring” category. This assessment is intended to serve as a validation step and test the robustness of the existing behavioral framework rather than redefine behavioral states or infer fine-scale foraging or energetic processes.

**Analytical Approach:**

Activity budgets will be estimated by quantifying the proportion of time individuals allocate to each behavioral state across deployments. Analyses will examine temporal variation in activity budgets over the course of the foraging season, as well as differences among demographic groups where sample sizes permit.

For the exploratory behavior assessment, dive metrics derived from PRH data (e.g., dive depth and duration) will be used descriptively to evaluate whether exploratory dives cluster into a small number of recurring types. This analysis is intentionally limited in scope and complexity, with the goal of informing interpretation rather than expanding the behavioral framework.

Detailed methodological decisions, parameter choices, and implementation specifics will be documented separately and are not the focus of this white paper.

**Expected Outcomes:**

-   Possible outcomes include the identification of broad dive groupings within exploratory behavior or the absence of strong clustering, which would support retaining “exploring” as a single behavioral state. Both outcomes are informative for interpreting baseline activity budgets.

-   Activity budgets are expected to vary both across the Antarctic foraging season and among demographic groups, reflecting changes in energetic state, prey availability, and life-history demands. In particular, we hypothesize that the proportion of time allocated to resting behavior will increase later in the foraging season, potentially corresponding to improved body condition following sustained foraging or shifts in prey distribution and accessibility. Concurrently, time spent foraging and exploring will likely decrease as individuals approach the end of the feeding period, which has been shown by (cite Ross). 

-   Demographic differences in activity budgets are also anticipated. For example, juveniles, lactating females, or other demographic classes may exhibit distinct patterns of time allocation reflecting differing energetic requirements or constraints. Characterizing these patterns provides important ecological context for interpreting baseline behavior and establishes a reference framework for evaluating how disturbance or environmental change may differentially affect individuals across the population.

    **Scope, Roles, and Contributions:**

This chapter is led and authored as part of my dissertation, with collaborators contributing through data collection, tag development, methodological guidance, and scientific discussion. All contributions will be acknowledged appropriately in the dissertation and any resulting publications.

Authorship on manuscripts arising from this work will be discussed transparently and in accordance with journal standards and community norms, based on the nature and extent of intellectual contribution beyond data provision and technical support.
